metadata.chiefComplaint: My left lower tooth hurts really badly when I bite, and it is hard to chew.
metadata.title: Necrotic Pulp with Acute Apical Periodontitis

findings.label: Location and tooth
findings.value: Left mandibular first molar with a large old filling/deep caries

findings.label: Cold test
findings.value: No current response to cold stimulus

findings.label: Percussion and biting
findings.value: Marked tenderness to percussion and biting pressure

findings.label: Gingiva
findings.value: Mild erythema and tenderness; no swelling and no fluctuance

findings.label: Drainage
findings.value: No pus, no purulence, and no sinus tract

findings.label: Airway
findings.value: Patent with normal voice, no drooling, and no compromise

examinations.title: Intraoral Examination Image
examinations.description: Left mandibular first molar and surrounding gingiva assessment.

conversation.openingGreeting: Hello. My left lower tooth hurts really badly when I bite.

scripted.response: Hello.
My left lower back tooth hurts really badly when I bite, and it is hard to chew.
It is the first big molar on the lower-left side.
This returned pain has been getting worse for five days, especially over the last 48 hours.
The tooth hurt severely about a week ago, then the pain stopped. It returned and has worsened over the last five days.
It hurt badly about a week ago, stopped, and then returned. I think an old filling may have broken or failed.
No, I do not remember biting a hard object. I only think the old filling may have broken or failed.
Yes, the pain is constant now.
It is a seven out of ten.
Biting, chewing, or tapping that tooth causes sharp, severe pain.
Cold hurt the tooth earlier in the illness.
Cold does not cause pain now, although it did earlier.
No, I have not had any swelling.
No, I have not noticed pus, drainage, or a bad taste.
No, I have not had fever or chills.
No, I have no trouble swallowing.
No, I am not drooling.
No, I have no trouble breathing.
Yes, I am allergic to penicillin.
Penicillin gives me hives.
I have taken ibuprofen 400 mg as needed, but it has not relieved the pain enough.
That lower-left first molar has a large old filling that may have broken. My last dental visit was about five years ago.
Yes, I want to save the tooth if possible.
I do not have dental insurance, so getting prompt dental care will take time to figure out.
I smoke about one pack per day.
The examination findings are available in the examination viewer.

patientChecklist.label: Opened the encounter respectfully
Asked what brought the patient in
Asked which tooth or side hurts
Asked about the five-day course
Asked about pain stopping, returning, and the old filling
Asked about constant pain and severity
Asked about biting, chewing, or tapping pain
Distinguished historical cold pain from current response
Asked about swelling
Asked about pus or drainage
Asked about fever or chills
Asked about swallowing, drooling, or breathing
Asked about penicillin allergy
Asked about the penicillin reaction
Asked about ibuprofen use and relief
Asked about the old filling and last dental visit
Asked about dental insurance or access
Asked whether the patient wants to save the tooth
Asked about smoking, alcohol, or drugs
Reviewed available examination findings

clinicalChecklist.label: Characterizes the five-day constant pain, sequence, severity, and biting aggravation
Elicits negative infection and airway symptoms
Elicits penicillin allergy, hives, ibuprofen use, and access barriers
Recognizes loss of cold response with percussion and biting tenderness
Diagnoses necrotic pulp with acute apical periodontitis

patientFacts.text: The painful tooth is the left mandibular first molar.
The returned pain has worsened over five days, especially during the last 48 hours.
Severe pain occurred about a week ago, stopped, then returned and worsened.
A large old filling on the left mandibular first molar may have broken or failed.
The patient does not recall biting a hard object or having a definite injury.
The current pain is constant.
The pain is rated 7/10.
Biting, chewing, and tapping cause sharp severe pain.
Cold hurt previously, earlier in the illness.
Cold does not cause pain now; formal absent response is an examination finding.
The patient has no swelling.
The patient has no pus or drainage.
The patient has no fever or chills.
The patient has no difficulty swallowing.
The patient is not drooling.
The patient has no difficulty breathing.
The patient is allergic to penicillin.
Penicillin causes hives.
The patient has taken ibuprofen 400 mg as needed without adequate relief.
The patient is otherwise healthy with no known opioid-use history and no ibuprofen contraindication.
The patient smokes approximately one pack per day.
The patient wants to save the tooth if possible.
The patient has no dental insurance, so prompt dental access will take time to arrange.

history.onset: Severe pain occurred about a week ago, stopped, and then returned; an old filling may have broken or failed.
history.duration: Five days of worsening returned pain, intensified during the last 48 hours.
history.pain: Constant 7/10 pain with sharp severe biting, chewing, and tapping pain; ibuprofen has not provided adequate relief.
history.medications: Ibuprofen 400 mg as needed without adequate relief.
history.medicalHistory: Otherwise healthy; no opioid-use history and no ibuprofen contraindication.
history.dentalHistory: Large old filling/deep caries on the left mandibular first molar; last dental visit about five years ago.
history.socialHistory: Smokes approximately one pack per day; occasional alcohol; no illicit drugs; no dental insurance and wants to save the tooth.

hpiFacts: Five days of worsening left mandibular first-molar pain after a prior severe episode stopped and returned.
Possible broken or failing old filling.
Constant 7/10 pain with sharp biting and chewing pain.
Cold caused pain earlier but no longer causes pain now.
No swelling, no drainage, no fever, no chills, no dysphagia, no drooling, and no dyspnea.
redFlags: No current swelling, drainage, fever, or systemic illness
Return for new swelling, fever, drainage, spreading infection, swallowing or breathing difficulty
examinationFindings: Large old filling/deep caries on the left mandibular first molar
No current response to cold testing
No swelling, fluctuance, pus, or sinus tract
Soft floor of mouth and patent airway
expectedQuestions: Where is the pain and how long has it been present?
Did severe pain stop and return?
Is the current pain constant and how severe is it?
Does biting, chewing, or tapping hurt?
Did cold hurt earlier and does it hurt now?
Any swelling, drainage, fever, chills, swallowing, drooling, or breathing problems?
Are you allergic to penicillin and what reaction occurs?
Can you obtain dental care and do you want to save the tooth?
supportingInfo.diagnosis: Necrotic pulp with acute apical periodontitis of the left mandibular first molar.
managementExpectations: Offer appropriate analgesia and local anesthesia or a dental block; the patient may decline the block.
Arrange urgent definitive dental treatment, ideally within approximately 72 hours.
Discuss root-canal treatment or extraction depending on dental evaluation and the patient's desire to save the tooth.
Explain that antibiotics are not currently indicated without swelling, drainage, fever, or systemic infection.
Provide reassessment and return precautions rather than requiring an unapproved delayed antibiotic prescription.
Address lack of dental insurance and access difficulty.
requiredInvestigations: Review structured oral examination and cold-test findings.
treatmentExpectations: Offer local anesthesia or dental block; patient may decline.
Urgent definitive root-canal treatment or extraction after dental evaluation.
No routine antibiotics for the current presentation.
referralExpectations: Urgent dental follow-up within approximately 72 hours.
Access planning for a patient without dental insurance.
safetyNettingExpectations: Seek reassessment for fever, swelling, drainage, spreading infection, systemic illness, dysphagia, or dyspnea.
learningObjectives: Connect prior severe pain cessation, absent cold response, and current percussion pain with pulpal necrosis and acute apical periodontitis.
Distinguish historical cold pain from clinician-observed current absent response.
Avoid routine antibiotics and plan definitive tooth-saving or extraction care.

keyFindings: Five-day constant 7/10 left mandibular first-molar pain after prior severe pain stopped and returned
Sharp biting, chewing, and percussion pain
Historical cold pain with current absent cold response
No current swelling, drainage, fever, or systemic infection
No dental insurance and desire to save the tooth
criticalMisses: Diagnosing cracked tooth or occlusal trauma as the primary condition
Missing necrotic pulp with acute apical periodontitis
Treating historical cold pain as current vitality
Recommending routine antibiotics without infection signs
Ignoring penicillin hives, access barriers, or the tooth-saving goal
reportData.idealSummary: A healthy 38-year-old with five days of constant 7/10 left mandibular first-molar pain after a prior severe episode stopped and returned, possible failure of a large old filling, historical cold pain but no current cold response, and marked biting/percussion tenderness without swelling, drainage, fever, or systemic infection, consistent with necrotic pulp and acute apical periodontitis requiring urgent definitive dental care rather than routine antibiotics.

facultyReview.question: Should future teaching include a delayed antibiotic prescription, or only reassessment and return precautions if infection signs develop? The delayed prescription remains unscored.
